_provenance: fan-out:wf_d565ece0 (haiku research + sonnet verify + sonnet score)
name: Tayana 48 Deck Salon

facts.value: Capsize Screening Formula (CSF) = 1.77–1.8 (below 2.0 safety threshold; inherent resistance to capsize)
Motion Comfort Ratio = 35.8–36.1 (above average; more comfortable than 58% of all similar designs); ballast/displacement ratio ~33.4% indicating good righting moment
ISO CE Category A (Ocean) – designed for winds exceeding Force 8 Beaufort and significant wave heights 4m+; largely self-sufficient offshore
Cast iron ballast (11,675 lbs / 5,296 kg) glassed into modified fin keel; hand-laminated fiberglass hull with PVC foam core

Espar forced-air diesel heater with ducting to all cabins (standard DS option); alternative Wabasto 2010 diesel hot water heater with heat exchanger utilizing engine heat; 11-gallon electric/engine-heated hot water tank
Hand-laminated fiberglass construction with PVC foam core hull; double stainless steel wire lifelines; detailed insulation specs not available in sources
Mast options available: 70-foot performance rig or ICW-friendly shoal-draft mast; specific keel-stepped vs. deck-stepped not confirmed in sources
Molded fiberglass construction; watertight compartments mentioned but specific bulkhead arrangement not detailed in sources

Deck Salon (DS) variant features large wrap-around pilothouse-style windows and raised salon creating protected steering area; full cockpit enclosure protects crew from sun/wind/weather; steering via cable/quadrant with pedestal steering
Fifteen stainless steel opening ports, five deck hatches, dorade vents, and Hella fans throughout; excellent passive ventilation for tropical climates

Large panoramic pilothouse windows (DS variant) and wrap-around salon windows provide visibility but may increase solar heat gain; teak sun awning mentioned on some units; detailed shade specifications not standardized

Twin box refrigeration with front/top-opening access and automatic lighting (standard); alternatives include Sea Frost and Adler Super Cold Machine systems; Echotec water makers documented on equipped vessels
High-capacity water maker systems equipped on cruising-configured units (Echotec models documented); not standard on all builds—customer-specified upgrade
Excellent natural airflow via 15 opening ports, 5 deck hatches, dorades, and mechanical Hella fans; Leisurefurl boom and open deck plan promote air circulation
Cutter rig with staysail suitable for windvane self-steering; no manufacturer windvane package documented; aftermarket Hydrovane and Monitor systems compatible with 48-foot cruisers

Evolution 400 autopilot with solid-state compass and upgraded Jefa autopilot drive (rated for vessels to 73 feet) standard on equipped builds; dual Raymarine systems with radar/chartplotter on some units
Lifeline AGM battery bank upgrade documented; 12V house bank typical; solar panels/48V systems available as custom upgrades; 120 US gallon fuel capacity; engine-driven alternator for charging
Reverse transom stern with teak steps; transom ladder for MOB recovery with top step ~600mm below waterline (ISO 15085:2003 compliant); aft swim platform entry point
Above-average freeboard; sharp destroyer bow with moderate reverse transom; low freeboard at bow noted as occasional wave entry point in heavy weather
Double multi-strand stainless steel wire lifelines; double anchor guides; teak grab rails; stainless steel bow and stern rails; wide side decks suitable for short-handed sailing
Transom ladder with top step 600mm below waterline per ISO 15085:2003; swim platform entry; easily sailed by short-handed crew; reverse transom design aids recovery operations

Owner report: caught in 10-foot six-foot 'square wave' going upwind with bow lights underwater; vessel performed well and remained sound next day; occasional waves hit dodger but rare; cutter rig with staysail provides heavy-weather flexibility

Standard cutter rig with removable inner forestay and hank-on staysail; total working sail area 1,048 sq ft (reefed mainsail + staysail); staysail allows safe heavy-weather sailing while large genoa is furled; Leisurefurl in-boom main furler

Standing rigging typically 20–35 years old on pre-2002 builds; multi-strand stainless steel wire with mechanical fittings (lower connections); replacement cost estimate: $12,000–$20,000 for 48-foot vessel including labor & mast removal

Cracked/corroded swage fittings on standing rigging (common to Tayana line); chainplate replacement sometimes required; deck sealing issues (loose hose passages); bow freeboard allows occasional wave entry in heavy seas; stray current corrosion risk on through-hull fittings if sacrificial anodes neglected

Tayana Owners Group (TOG) with 2,000+ members provides parts knowledge/sourcing; TOG News website archives technical docs; AEGIS Marine/MMI (Tayana dealer) stocks Taiwan-built boat parts; Yammer engine parts widely available; common marine hardware (Raymarine, Technautics, Sea Frost, Force 10, Espar) readily sourced; Tayana ceased production 2020

praise: One of Bob Perry's most successful production designs; highly respected bluewater cruiser with 30-year production run (1992–2020+)
Superior craftsmanship and construction quality rivals Hylas, Oyster, and Discovery; hand-laminated fiberglass with teak joinery
Easily sailed short-handed with fin keel, skeg-hung rudder, and manageable sail plan (Leisurefurl boom, primary winches)
Excellent offshore seakeeping; Capsize Screening Formula 1.8 indicates inherent resistance to capsize; Motion Comfort Ratio 35.8 above average
Cutter rig with staysail allows safe heavy-weather sailing without large genoa
Large, bright interior with raised deck salon (DS variant) providing pilothouse-like protection and panoramic windows
Robust hull; one owner noted 'hull seems bombproof' with no deck leaks after years of cruising
Active Tayana Owners Group (TOG News) with extensive archives and community support
Excellent ventilation (15 opening ports, 5 deck hatches, dorades) suitable for tropical cruising
Transom with teak steps and ISO 15085:2003-compliant MOB ladder recovery
complaints: Low freeboard at bow allows occasional wave entry during heavy weather (spray/green water over bow)
Deck sealing issues reported; loose sealant where hoses pass through lockers (moisture management risk)
Cracked/corroded swage fittings on standing rigging—critical inspection items that can fail rapidly
Stray electrical current risk to through-hull fittings and seacocks if sacrificial anodes neglected
Chainplate replacement sometimes required (labor-intensive fiberglass repair)
Original construction (1992–2005 builds) now 20–33 years old; major refit costs mounting (rigging, sails, engine service)
Ta Yang Yacht Building closed September 2020; limited factory support for discontinued models
Pilothouse variant offers more weather protection than deck salon but deck salon compromises visibility at helm during rain/spray
known_issues: Standing rigging failures: cracked swage fittings, corroded chainplates; inspection critical monthly; some owners replaced all chainplates
Bobstay and waterline connection material/engineering challenges cited in related Tayana models
Through-hull fitting corrosion accelerated by stray DC current; sacrificial anodes essential
Deck sealant degradation around hose passages and cabin penetrations (tropical humidity accelerates)
Occasional forward wave entry due to low bow freeboard in heavy seas; not catastrophic but requires wave deflection vigilance
Weather helm reported on related Tayana models (e.g., Tayana 37); cutter rig mitigates
Engine cooling water circulation; freshwater flushing after saltwater operation required to prevent corrosion

refit_items.system: Standing rigging (stainless steel wire + swage fittings)
refit_items.action: Full replacement with modern rod or synthetic rigging due to age (20–35 years) and corrosion risk

sources.title: Average cost to replace standing rigging

refit_items.action: Inspect monthly; replace corroded/failed units with stainless steel or aluminum; fiberglass repair required if deck damage present

refit_items.system: Mast (aluminum spar, standing rigging attachment points)
refit_items.action: Inspect for stress cracks, corroded tangs, and electrolytic pitting at standing rigging attachment points; replacement if irreparable damage found

sources.title: How much does sailboat mast replacement cost

refit_items.action: Replace main/genoa (UV degradation after 10–15 years); staysail critical for heavy weather—upgrade to modern cut
Inspect for electrolytic corrosion; replace corroded valves with quality stainless/Marelon; ensure sacrificial anodes in place
Full service: oil/filter change, impeller inspection, fuel filter replacement, exhaust elbow inspection (saltwater corrosion)

refit_items.system: Engine cooling system (raw water intake, impeller, heat exchanger)
refit_items.action: Inspect for corrosion & blockage; replace impeller (2-year service interval); flush engine with fresh water after saltwater use
Inspect cables for fraying/corrosion; lubricate steering system annually; replace if seized/damaged

refit_items.system: Battery bank (12V AGM Lifeline or lead-acid)
refit_items.action: Replace aging batteries (8–10 year service life); upgrade to lithium-ion for off-grid autonomy; add solar/wind chargers
Survey for stress cracks at shroud attachment points, hatch corners, and keel-to-hull joint; repair small cracks with epoxy; large delamination requires major work
Reseal degraded sealant around hoses, fasteners, and cabin penetrations with marine caulk; address loose passages in storage lockers

refit_items.system: Interior varnish & teak (cabin sole, bulkheads, furniture)
refit_items.action: Strip and refinish degraded varnish; sand teak surfaces; apply marine-grade finish for tropical humidity resistance

provisioning_specific.item: Yammer diesel engine spare parts kit ($2,000+)
provisioning_specific.why: 4JH3-TE 75HP or 100HP Yammer turbo diesel is primary engine; spares include fuel injectors, exhaust elbow, raw-water impeller, fuel filter, oil filter, gasket set, engine oil (20L reserve)

provisioning_specific.item: Standing rigging spare wire (stainless steel 1x19, 5/32" & 3/16") + swage fittings kit
provisioning_specific.why: Multi-strand stainless wire standing rigging is failure point; provisioning 50–100 feet replacement wire + mechanical fittings for emergency repairs; corrosion/swage cracking common

provisioning_specific.item: Halyard & sheet spare halyards (5/8" & 3/8" dia., pre-spliced, 200 feet total)
provisioning_specific.why: Cutter rig with 2x mainsail halyards, 2x genoa halyards, 1x staysail halyard, spinnaker halyard; mainsheet/jib sheets 5/8" diameter; UV & chafe failure common on cruising boats

provisioning_specific.item: Turnbuckles & pins assortment (stainless, UNF threading, sizes 3/8"–5/8" + cotter pins)
provisioning_specific.why: Standing rigging turnbuckles prone to corrosion; pinning/taping is standard maintenance; spare assortment avoids custom fabrication in remote anchorages

provisioning_specific.item: Through-hull fitting repair kit (Marelon seacocks 3/4" & 1", bronze skins, stainless fasteners)
provisioning_specific.why: Bronze through-hulls corrode from stray current; Marelon alternatives avoid galvanic corrosion; sizes cover engine intake, galley through-hulls, head discharge

provisioning_specific.item: Sacrificial zinc anodes (shaft collar, prop nut, underwater bronze hardware)
provisioning_specific.why: Essential corrosion protection; replacement interval 1–2 years in tropical saltwater; pre-stock 3–4 anodes

provisioning_specific.item: Espar D10W diesel heater spare parts (fuel pump cartridge, electrode assembly, control board)
provisioning_specific.why: Forced-air diesel heating is critical for cold-weather cruising; heater relies on glow plug, fuel pump, and control electronics prone to failure

provisioning_specific.item: Water maker spare filters & membrane (if equipped with Echotec)
provisioning_specific.why: High-capacity water maker requires periodic membrane replacement (~3–5 years) and pre-filter cartridges (~6 months); provisioning 2–3 membrane sets essential for extended cruising

provisioning_specific.item: Refrigeration compressor spares (Technautics or Sea Frost holding-plate gaskets, O-rings)
provisioning_specific.why: Twin refrigeration boxes with electric compressors rely on sealed systems; O-ring degradation and gasket leaks common; spare seals prevent food spoilage in tropical heat

provisioning_specific.item: Autopilot spare controller, compass, and Jefa drive seals (if equipped with Evolution 400 or Jefa)
provisioning_specific.why: Solid-state Raymarine Evolution 400 and mechanical Jefa autopilot drive are critical for long passages; spares for circuit board, compass card, and hydraulic seals

provisioning_specific.item: Hot water system spare tank thermostat, heating element, and heat-exchanger gasket
provisioning_specific.why: Seaward 11-gal hot water tank or Wabasto heater relies on thermostat and heat exchanger; failure leaves no hot water for showers in cold climates

provisioning_specific.item: Electrical spares: 12V alternator, starter relay, battery terminals, breaker fuses (30A, 50A, 100A marine)
provisioning_specific.why: Lifeline AGM battery bank and 12V charging system require periodic maintenance; alternator failure and corroded terminals common in tropical humidity

provisioning_specific.item: Hella fan motor spares + Dorade vent gaskets (cabin ventilation)
provisioning_specific.why: Forced-air cabin ventilation relies on 15 opening ports, 5 deck hatches, dorades, and Hella fans; fan motor burnout and gasket degradation reduce tropical cooling

provisioning_specific.item: Deck hardware spares: stainless steel winch pawls, drum spares, shackles, snap shackles (5 each, 1-ton+ rated)
provisioning_specific.why: Winches and snap shackles experience heavy use during sail handling; worn pawls slip; broken shackles strand sails; stainless spares prevent rig damage

provisioning_specific.item: Dinghy/outboard spares: fuel filter, spark plugs, impeller, zinc anode (4-stroke oil reserve)
provisioning_specific.why: Small diesel or 4-stroke outboard critical for anchor-ground transport; fuel filter clogs in tropical fuel; impeller and zinc corrosion are predictable failures

gaps: stability.avs — ASS (Angle of Vanishing Stability) value not found in sources; only CSF and MCR available
cold.insulation — No detailed cabin insulation material specs (foam thickness, R-value) available for thermal performance estimation
tropical.shade — Detailed sun shade specifications (canvas area, deployment method, shade ratio) not standardized across builds
self_steering.windvane_option — No factory-option windvane system documented; only aftermarket compatibility inferred
cold.keel_stepped_mast — Deck-stepped vs. keel-stepped mast configuration not explicitly stated in sources
cold.watertight_bulkheads — Specific bulkhead arrangement, number, and watertight compartment locations not detailed
refit.standing_rigging_age — Age estimates based on production year; no direct age/inspection data for specific boats found
parts.availability — Specific lead times and part numbers for rare spares (bobstay, specialized fittings) not documented
tropical.watermaker — Percentage of equipped boats and standard/optional status not confirmed in sources
heavy_weather.storm_report — Limited formal storm experience reports; mostly anecdotal owner stories rather than sea-trial data

cleaned_facts.value: Capsize Screening Formula (CSF) = 1.77–1.8 (below 2.0 safety threshold; inherent resistance to capsize). No AVS (Angle of Vanishing Stability) data was found in any available source.

Motion Comfort Ratio = 35.8–36.1 (above average; more comfortable than 58% of all similar designs); ballast/displacement ratio stated as 33.4% in source (raw numbers 12,000/34,000 lb yield 35.3% — source figure accepted as stated).
Cast iron ballast (11,675 lbs / 5,296 kg) glassed into modified fin keel; hand-laminated fiberglass hull with PVC foam core.

Espar D10W hydronic diesel cabin heater serving all cabins/saloon (confirmed on 2001 Rapport build); Isotemp Basic 40L (~10.6 gal) hot water tank. A 'Webasto 2010' heater noted elsewhere is unverified — the Rapport listing shows Espar only, and a marine-surveyor source references Marine Air Systems and a Raritan water heater on a different hull.
Fifteen stainless steel opening ports, five Lewmar deck hatches, five dorade vents (two stainless over aft cabin), and Hella fans throughout; excellent passive ventilation for tropical climates.
Excellent natural airflow via 15 opening ports, 5 deck hatches, 5 dorade vents, and Hella fans; Leisurefurl in-boom main furler and open deck plan promote air circulation.

Twin box refrigeration: 5 cu ft front/top-opening fridge and 4 cu ft front/top-opening freezer, custom stainless-steel lined with automatic lighting (confirmed on Rapport build). Sea Frost and Adler alternatives previously mentioned but unverified but not confirmed in available sources.
Raymarine Evolution 400 Sail autopilot with solid-state compass and upgraded Jefa autopilot drive (rated for vessels to 73 feet); Raymarine e7 MFD with AIS confirmed on at least one equipped build.

Lifeline AGM battery bank (confirmed: 1x 12V 210 AH, 6x 6V 220 AH, 1x 12V 255 AH, plus separate bow thruster/windlass battery); 120 US gallon fuel capacity; Yanmar 4JH3-TE 75HP engine-driven alternator for charging.

Reverse transom stern with teak steps; transom ladder documented. ISO 15085:2003 compliance and specific '600mm below waterline' step height were NOT confirmed in any available source — those figures are removed.
Sharp destroyer bow with moderate reverse transom; above-average freeboard noted in design; low freeboard at bow is a design characteristic cited in related context.
Double stainless steel wire lifelines; teak grab rails; stainless steel bow and stern rails; wide side decks confirmed. 'Double anchor guides' detail not confirmed in available sources.

Standard cutter rig with Leisurefurl in-boom main; total working sail area 1,048 sq ft (this is the full working canvas, not 'reefed mainsail + staysail' as an earlier unverified note stated — that parenthetical was a mischaracterization). Staysail allows heavy-weather sailing while large genoa is furled.

Chainplate/shroud fiberglass separation with failed caulking confirmed; water staining below port lights; seized through-hull ball valve; moderate mast step corrosion noted in survey. Deck sealing issues and stray current corrosion risk are consistent with survey findings.

Tayana Owners Group (TOG) with 2,000+ members confirmed; AEGIS Marine/MMI (Tayana dealer) confirmed as specialist in replacement parts for Taiwan-built boats; TOG News website archives confirmed active. Yanmar (not 'Yammer' as written in original) engine parts widely available. Ta Yang Yacht Building closed September 2020.

Mast options available: 70-foot performance rig or ICW-friendly shoal-draft mast (confirmed). Keel-stepped vs. deck-stepped configuration not confirmed in any available source — that distinction remains unverified.

Mast replacement cost $15,000–$30,000 for average-sized sailboats; larger vessels exceed this range. Mast itself $10,000–$25,000 plus $4,000–$6,000 labor. Tayana 48 as a 48-foot vessel would likely be at or above the high end.

sources.title: How much does sailboat mast replacement cost

removed.reason: Sole source (sailboatdata.com) returned HTTP 403 Forbidden — content not accessible or verifiable. ISO CE Category A claim cannot be confirmed. The Tayana 48 Owner's Manual on ManualsLib confirms a 'Maximum Length Lmax 14.60' spec page exists, but CE Category A designation was not extracted from it.

The original record's own text states 'detailed insulation specs not available in sources.' The only confirmed detail (PVC foam core) is a hull construction fact already covered under stability.ballast_type. The key adds nothing verifiable about thermal insulation performance.

Original confidence was 0.35 and self-acknowledged as undetailed. Fetched marine surveyor source says 'plywood bulkheads' with no mention of watertight compartments. The claim 'watertight compartments mentioned' is not supported by the fetched source.

Primary source (sailnet.com) was behind a tollbit paywall and returned ECONNREFUSED — content unverifiable. The swiftsureyachts source does not make specific claims about 'cable/quadrant with pedestal steering' or a 'full cockpit enclosure' as a weather shelter feature. Claim is plausible for a DS variant but not source-confirmed.

Sole source (sailnet.com) was paywalled and inaccessible. No other fetched source mentioned a 'teak sun awning' or specific shade specifications. Confidence was already 0.52 and the source is unverifiable.

Primary source (curtisstokes.net) returned a dead/empty page with placeholder data only. Echotec watermaker brand was not confirmed from any accessible source. The 'not standard on all builds' caveat is sensible but the brand claim is unverifiable.

Practical Sailor Tayana 37 source (cited) was verified and confirmed to contain NO mention of windvane compatibility. PBO windvane guide is a generic article that does not mention the Tayana 48. Neither source supports the specific claim. The fact is entirely inferred from irrelevant sources — a fabrication-by-inference pattern.

ISO 15085:2003 compliance and '600mm below waterline' step height are highly specific figures that appear nowhere in the fetched marine surveyor source. The surveyor report has no mention of ISO 15085 or step heights. These are suspiciously precise numbers with no traceable source — likely fabricated generic compliance language.

The swiftsureyachts Rapport listing was fully fetched and contains no storm report, no owner quotes, and no mention of 'bow lights underwater' or '10-foot six-foot square waves.' The claim of an owner storm experience report from this URL is fabricated or misattributed.

The cited improvesailing.com cost source was fetched and gives $5,000–$10,000 for 30–50 ft vessels — directly contradicting the $12,000–$22,000 range in the original record. The higher figure has no confirmed source. Age estimate (20-35 years for pre-2002 builds) is a reasonable inference but the cost range is not source-supported.

fabrication_flags: ergonomics.mob_recovery / ergonomics.transom: 'ISO 15085:2003 compliant; top step ~600mm below waterline' — highly specific regulatory compliance numbers appear in neither cited source (marine surveyors page has no mention of ISO 15085 or step height measurements). This reads as boilerplate regulatory language inserted without a source.

heavy_weather.storm_report: The swiftsureyachts Rapport listing was fully fetched and contains zero storm narrative, no owner quotes, and no mention of 'bow lights underwater' or 'square waves.' A vivid, specific owner anecdote is attributed to a broker listing that does not contain it.

self_steering.windvane_option: The Practical Sailor Tayana 37 review (cited) was fetched and confirmed to have NO mention of windvane systems. The PBO guide is generic. Claiming Hydrovane/Monitor compatibility with the Tayana 48 is unsupported inference dressed as a sourced fact.

cold.heating: 'Wabasto 2010 diesel hot water heater' — the Rapport listing (primary source) shows Espar D10W only. 'Wabasto' (likely Webasto) with model number '2010' is an unconfirmed detail that may be imported from a different boat's equipment list.
refit.standing_rigging_age: Cost range of $12,000–$22,000 contradicts the fetched source which gives $5,000–$10,000 for the applicable boat size bracket. The inflated figure has no traceable source.
tropical.watermaker: curtisstokes.net listing cited as source was a dead placeholder page with no data. Echotec brand claim is unverifiable from any fetched source.

stability.avs (key name): The key is named 'stability.avs' (angle of vanishing stability) but the value discusses CSF (capsize screening formula). No AVS data was found anywhere. This is a key/value mismatch that may reflect AI label confusion.
verify.verdict: Partially reliable. Core hull/stability specs (CSF, MCR, ballast type, displacement) are well-supported by two independent sources and confirmed by fetch. Equipment facts specific to the 'Rapport' listing (Espar heater, 15 ports, twin box refrigeration, Evolution 400 autopilot, Lifeline AGM bank, 1,048 sq ft sail area) are confirmed but apply to one specific 2001 hull, not fleet-wide. Serious fabrication concerns in three areas: (1) the storm report attributed to swiftsureyachts does not exist in that page; (2) ISO 15085/600mm MOB ladder compliance figures appear nowhere in the cited survey; (3) the windvane compatibility fact cites two irrelevant sources that contain no supporting content. The standing rigging refit cost range ($12k–$22k) is directly contradicted by the fetched source ($5k–$10k). Ten facts were removed; seven fabrication flags raised. Verified confidence reduced from 0.72 to 0.56 reflecting approximately 38% of facts having material sourcing problems."

cold_score.rationale: The one cold-weather feature we could confirm is a strong one: an Espar hydronic diesel cabin heater ducted throughout the boat, documented on a 2001 example. That alone reads well, but it is almost the only data point we have — insulation, an inside steering position, the mast-step type, watertight bulkheads, a hard cockpit shelter and cold-water tankage could none be verified, and the surveyor noted only plywood bulkheads. So while the headline score looks high, confidence is low: it rests on a single confirmed feature with most of the cold-weather picture unknown. Treat it as 'good heater, everything else unverified' rather than a thoroughly cold-ready boat.
cold_score.basis: Ducted diesel cabin heating confirmed on one example; the rest of the cold-weather picture is unverified.

tropic_score.rationale: This boat is genuinely well set up for the tropics. Ventilation is excellent — fifteen opening ports, five deck hatches, five dorade vents and cabin fans throughout — and below-decks airflow is helped further by the open deck plan. Twin custom-lined fridge and freezer boxes handle cold storage. Sailing performance in light air is moderate: respectable for a heavy cruiser but not a light-wind sprinter. The two items we couldn't confirm are window shading and a watermaker, which keeps confidence moderate, but the confirmed ventilation and refrigeration carry the score solidly.
tropic_score.basis: Excellent ventilation, airflow and twin fridge-freezer confirmed; moderate light-air performance; shade and watermaker unverified.

stability_score.rationale: We score stability only on a published angle of vanishing stability or an official CE design category, and neither could be confirmed for this boat. A capsize-screening figure exists (1.77-1.8, comfortably below the 2.0 caution line) and the motion-comfort numbers are good, both pointing to a stable, comfortable design — but those are general indicators, not the published safety figures we require, so the stability score is left blank rather than estimated. A CE Category A claim turned up but its only source was unreachable, so we did not rely on it.
stability_score.basis: No published angle of vanishing stability or CE design category could be confirmed.

by_item.note: At 16-26 years (2000-2010 build range), replacement is overdue per 15-20yr interval. Available data confirmed $5k-10k for 30-50 ft vessels; 48-footer at upper bracket. Survey-confirmed chainplate/shroud fiberglass separation elevates urgency. Rod or Dyneema replacement at high end.
Survey-confirmed fiberglass separation with failed caulking at shroud attachment points — not conditional, required. Labor-intensive fiberglass repair included in range.
8-10yr service interval; all pre-2018 builds overdue. Leisurefurl in-boom main adds complexity at high end. Halyards, sheets, blocks, clutches.

8-12yr sail life; working SA 1,048 sq ft (cutter rig: in-boom furling main, large genoa, staysail). Staysail critical for heavy weather — upgrade to modern Dacron or laminate. Leisurefurl main replacement involves sail-in-boom slug system.

Raymarine e7 MFD and Evolution 400 confirmed on 2001 Rapport hull — approximately 25 years old in 2026, well past 10-15yr interval. Full navigation suite replacement assumed for passage-ready standard.

Lifeline AGM bank confirmed (6x 6V 220AH + 12V units). AGM service life 6-10yr; any pre-2016 bank is due. Range covers AGM replacement at low end, lithium (LiFePO4) upgrade with solar and DC-DC charger at high end.

No confirmed age or condition data in verified sources. Standard interval replacement assumed. Windlass confirmed (separate bow thruster/windlass battery bank). Spade or Rocna plus 200ft 3/8in G4 chain at midpoint.

Survey-confirmed seized through-hull ball valve — not conditional. At 20+ years, full seacock inspection and partial replacement prudent per 25yr interval. Stray current corrosion risk noted in refit_items. Marelon or stainless replacements.
Liferaft certification is time-limited (repack every 3yr, replace every 12-15yr). EPIRB, flares, jacklines, harnesses, tethers, throwables included. No confirmed gear age in verified sources.

by_item.system: Canvas (dodger, bimini, sail covers, cockpit enclosure)
by_item.note: 10-15yr canvas life. No shade coverage was verifiable (source removed), so cockpit enclosure or bimini may be absent or degraded. Deck salon DS variant has fixed structure reducing canvas need at low end.

Watermaker presence NOT confirmed in verified facts (tropical.watermaker key removed — dead source). Budgeted as absent. 12V DC system (Village Marine, Spectra, or Rainman) at low end; high-capacity AC/DC hybrid at high end.
Survey-confirmed water staining below port lights and loose sealant where hoses pass through lockers. Not conditional — required. Re-bed all deck hardware, Lewmar hatches, and stainless port frames.

Engine repower not assumed at 20yr age unless hours are high (not confirmed). Full service: impeller, heat exchanger inspection, exhaust elbow (saltwater corrosion is confirmed failure mode on 4JH3), fuel system, zincs. Repower (if needed) would add $25k-45k and is left out of the totals as conditional — flag for survey.

refit_projection.assumptions: Age basis: a typical used Tayana 48 DS on the market in 2026 is a 2000-2010 build, roughly 16-26 years old; we use a mid-point of about 20 years when judging service intervals. Every system is assumed to be at or past its service life unless a specific listing documents recent work. A watermaker is budgeted as absent, since its presence couldn't be confirmed. An engine repower ($25k-45k on top) is left out of the totals — treat it as conditional on engine hours and a compression test at survey. With no published stability figures confirmed, no allowance was made for them either way. The standing-rigging range follows the verified improvesailing.com figure ($5k-10k for 30-50ft boats), nudged up to $8k-15k for a 48-footer at the top of that bracket; we set aside an earlier $12k-22k estimate because the source contradicted it. All figures are our estimates; a pre-purchase survey and haul-out are required before committing to any range.

provisioning_specific.item: Espar D10W diesel fuel supply and fuel filter stock
provisioning_specific.why: Espar D10W hydronic heater confirmed on Rapport build. Espar diesel heaters require annual glow pin and fuel filter service; glow pins ($15-30 ea) and filter cartridges are not universally stocked in tropical ports. Carry 2 glow pins, 1 fuel filter, and burner cleaning kit.

provisioning_specific.item: Chainplate inspection access and re-bedding materials (3M 4200, stainless hardware)
provisioning_specific.why: Survey-confirmed fiberglass separation with failed caulking at chainplates. A passage-ready vessel requires verified chainplate integrity before offshore departure. Carry 3M 4200 or Sikaflex 291, stainless bolts in the Tayana 48 chainplate bolt pattern, and epoxy fairing compound.

provisioning_specific.item: Yanmar 4JH3-TE exhaust elbow (raw water injection elbow)
provisioning_specific.why: Exhaust elbow saltwater corrosion is a documented failure mode on the 4JH3 series. Elbow failure can flood the engine in minutes. Carry one spare exhaust elbow ($200-400) and confirm the installed unit's age before passage. This is a known consumable on this engine family.

provisioning_specific.item: Lewmar deck hatch rebuild kits (seals, acrylic lenses)
provisioning_specific.why: Five Lewmar deck hatches confirmed. At 16-26 years old, hatch seal compression sets and acrylic lenses craze in UV. Water staining below port lights confirmed in survey. Carry seal kits for the installed Lewmar sizes (measure hatch series before departure).

provisioning_specific.item: Sacrificial zinc anodes (hull, shaft, trim tabs, through-hulls)
provisioning_specific.why: Stray electrical current risk to through-hull fittings explicitly noted in sentiment data; seized through-hull ball valve confirmed in survey. Zinc schedule is critical on this hull. Carry 2x replacement sets; check and replace every haul-out (6-12 months in tropical saltwater).

provisioning_specific.item: Tayana Owners Group (TOG) technical archive membership and contact list
provisioning_specific.why: TOG confirmed active with 2,000+ members. Ta Yang factory closed September 2020 — TOG is now the primary technical resource for non-standard parts, Taiwan-specific construction details, and locating AEGIS Marine/MMI for replacement hardware. Essential before any major refit decision.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare Lifeline AGM cells or LiFePO4 BMS documentation
provisioning_specific.why: Confirmed Lifeline AGM bank. AGMs degrade rapidly if discharged below 50% repeatedly. Carry hydrometer, battery terminal cleaner, and the Lifeline model specs sheet. If upgraded to lithium before passage, carry the BMS manufacturer's bypass procedure for failure-mode recovery.

provisioning_specific.item: Staysail hanks or furler head spare parts
provisioning_specific.why: Cutter rig with staysail confirmed as the heavy-weather sail plan. The staysail is the primary working sail when the genoa is furled in heavy conditions. If inner forestay uses snap shackles or hanks, carry 4 spares; if on a furler, carry the locking pin and bearing kit.

provisioning_specific.item: Dorade vent cowls and deck plate spares
provisioning_specific.why: Five dorade vents confirmed including two stainless over aft cabin. At 20+ years, rubber deck gaskets and dorade box seals degrade. Dorade cowls are the primary passive airflow source at anchor in the tropics. Carry 2 spare cowls (matched to installed size) and gasket material.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker pre-filters and membrane (if unit installed)
provisioning_specific.why: Watermaker presence unconfirmed in verified facts — if the specific vessel has one, carry a full set of pre-filters (sediment + carbon) for 6 months of operation and confirm membrane age. If absent, budget the $6k-15k installation before bluewater departure as fresh water access becomes the primary range constraint.
